seoTitle: How to Enable Cache Everything in Cloudflare
metaDescription: Force full-page HTML caching in Cloudflare with a Cache Rule, set a custom Edge TTL, and exclude sensitive paths like login and checkout.
intro: By default, Cloudflare only caches static assets, but sometimes you want dynamic HTML pages cached too for maximum speed. This guide covers building a Cache Everything rule while making sure sensitive areas like login and checkout stay excluded.

steps.heading: Open Cache Rules
steps.body: Navigate to the Caching sub-directory and open the dedicated Cache Rules panel.

steps.heading: Create a new rule
steps.body: Use the Expression Builder to isolate the specific domain and path names you want cached.

steps.heading: Set Cache Eligibility to Eligible
steps.body: In the rule settings, toggle the Cache Eligibility option to override standard media-only limits and force full-page caching.

steps.heading: Set an Edge TTL
steps.body: Define a distinct Edge Cache TTL duration to control how long Cloudflare's servers retain the cached HTML copies.

steps.heading: Exclude sensitive paths and deploy
steps.body: Stack exclusion rules to bypass caching on login pages, checkout portals, or authenticated sessions, then deploy and audit response headers to confirm cache hits.

faq.question: Is it safe to cache dynamic HTML pages?
faq.answer: It's safe as long as you exclude pages with personalized or sensitive content, like login and checkout flows, using bypass rules.

faq.question: What does Cache Eligibility do in a Cache Rule?
faq.answer: It overrides Cloudflare's default behavior of only caching static file types, allowing dynamic HTML responses to be cached as well.

faq.question: How do I make sure logged-in users see fresh, personalized content?
faq.answer: Use cookie-based bypass rules within your Cache Rules to keep authenticated sessions dynamic and excluded from caching.

faq.question: How can I confirm Cache Everything is actually working?
faq.answer: Run a header audit on a cached page and look for a cache status header indicating a hit rather than a miss.
